title: Memory, Threads & Processes
subtitle: Stack vs heap, processes vs threads, and blocking vs non-blocking

sections.title: Stack vs Heap Memory
sections.text:
Your program's memory is like a desk (stack) and a warehouse (heap).

Stack — Your desk. Small, fast, organized. When you call a function, you put what you need on the desk. When the function finishes, you clear it. This happens automatically and instantly. But space is limited (~1-8 MB per thread).

Heap — The warehouse. Big, shared, slower. You store objects and complex data here. You control when things go in and come out. In JavaScript/Python/Go, the garbage collector cleans up for you. In C, you do it yourself with malloc/free.

Stack overflow happens when you put too much on the desk (think infinite recursion). Memory leaks happen when you fill the warehouse and forget to take things out.

items: Thread-local — each thread has its own

sections.caption: Stack vs heap: two fundamentally different memory regions with different tradeoffs

sections.title: Processes vs Threads
sections.text:
A process is a running program — like a whole restaurant with its own kitchen, staff, and entrance. Processes are isolated: if one crashes, others keep running.

A thread is one worker inside that restaurant. Multiple threads share the same kitchen (memory) and can communicate instantly, but they can also step on each other's toes (race conditions).

Node.js uses one thread with an event loop (one super-efficient waiter). Java uses many threads (many waiters). Go uses goroutines (lightweight virtual waiters). Each approach has tradeoffs, but they all handle thousands of users.
sections.caption: Processes are isolated and heavyweight; threads are shared and lightweight
sections.detail: The choice between processes and threads depends on your workload. CPU-bound work benefits from multiple processes (to avoid GIL in Python) or threads (in languages with true parallelism like Go). I/O-bound work benefits from async/await or event loops regardless of thread count.
sections.callout: Node.js uses a single-threaded event loop + worker pool for async I/O. Go uses goroutines (lightweight threads). Python uses threads but they're limited by the GIL for CPU work.

sections.title: Blocking vs Non-Blocking
sections.text:
Blocking is like a cashier who stops breathing until the customer finishes paying. The thread sits idle, doing nothing, waiting for the database/file/API to respond. This wastes time.

Non-blocking is like a cashier who says "I'll check on that order later" and helps the next customer while the kitchen cooks. The thread starts an operation, moves on to other work, and comes back when the result is ready.

This is why Node.js can handle thousands of connections with a single thread — it never waits. When your code does await db.query(), the thread doesn't sit there — it goes and processes another request.

items: Thread waits for I/O to complete

sections.caption: Non-blocking I/O allows a single thread to handle thousands of concurrent connections
